# Part 1 — Data Acquisition, Cleaning, and Exploratory Analysis

## Dataset description and justification

**Dataset:** synthetic "Employee Compensation & Performance" dataset (2,000 rows, generated by `generate_raw_data.py`).

This project environment has no internet access, so instead of downloading an external file, the dataset is generated programmatically with realistic, documented relationships (e.g. salary driven by experience, education, department, and performance, with random noise) and deliberately-injected data-quality issues (nulls, duplicates, a messy dtype column, and skewed/outlier-prone columns). The generation logic in `generate_raw_data.py` is the "data dictionary" for this dataset — every column's meaning and how it was constructed is documented there. Using a generated dataset means every number reported below is verifiable by re-running `python3 generate_raw_data.py && python3 eda_pipeline.py`.

**Columns:**
| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| employee_id | numeric (int) | row identifier |
| age | numeric | employee age, 22–60 |
| years_experience | numeric | years of work experience (correlated with age) |
| education_level | categorical, **ordinal** | High School < Bachelors < Masters < PhD |
| department | categorical, **nominal** | Sales / Engineering / Marketing / HR / Finance / Support |
| region | categorical, **nominal** | North / South / East / West / Central |
| performance_score | numeric | 1–5 performance rating |
| monthly_hours | numeric | hours worked per month |
| distance_from_office_km | numeric | commute distance |
| num_projects | numeric | active project count |
| bonus_pct | numeric | bonus as % of salary — **dropped**, see Task 2 |
| **salary** | numeric | **regression target** |

The dataset meets the brief's minimum requirements: 2,000 rows (≥500), 7 numeric columns (≥5), 3 categorical columns (≥2), with `salary` as a clean continuous regression target that is binarized at its median in Part 2 for the classification target.

Satisfies requirement (c): repository contains the generation script (equivalent to a "download instruction" — running it reproduces the exact raw file, seeded with `random_state=42`).

---

## Task 2 — Null value analysis

| Column | Null count | Null % |
|---|---|---|
| performance_score | 140 | 6.95% |
| monthly_hours | 101 | 5.01% |
| distance_from_office_km | 123 | 6.10% |
| **bonus_pct** | **705** | **34.99%** |
| all other columns | 0 | 0.00% |

**`bonus_pct` exceeds the 20% threshold** and was **dropped** rather than imputed. Reasoning: at ~35% missing, any single imputed value (mean, median, or a model-based fill) would be standing in for over a third of the column's information — that's no longer "filling a few gaps," it's substituting a guess for a third of the data, which risks injecting a false signal into any model trained on it later. The other three columns are all under 20% and were filled with the column **median**.

**Why median, not mean:** the mean is pulled toward whichever tail has more extreme values (see the skewness discussion below) — a handful of very high `distance_from_office_km` values pull the mean up well past where most employees actually sit, so filling nulls with the mean would systematically overstate typical values. The median is the middle of the sorted data and isn't dragged around by outliers, so it's a more representative "typical value" to fill gaps with, especially before outliers have been analyzed (Task 6).

---

## Task 3 — Duplicate detection and removal

**15 duplicate rows** were found (`df.duplicated().sum() == 15`) and removed, taking the dataset from 2,015 → 2,000 rows. Since the duplicates were exact row copies of already-complete records (none of the source rows had nulls in the columns retained after Task 2), removing them **did not change any column's null percentage** — all columns show 0.0% before and after.

---

## Task 4 — Data type correction

- **`years_experience`** loaded as an **object/string** column (dtype `str`) because ~1.5% of raw entries were recorded as the placeholder `"unknown"` instead of a number — a realistic messy-export artifact. Converting with `pd.to_numeric(df['years_experience'], errors='coerce')` correctly turned those 30 placeholder entries into `NaN`, which were then median-filled (18.4 years). Final dtype: `float64`.
- **`department`, `education_level`, `region`** were repetitive string columns converted to **`category`** dtype.
- **Memory usage:** 569,148 bytes (555.8 KB) → 150,831 bytes (147.3 KB), a **73.5% reduction**. The bulk of the saving comes from `category` dtype storing each repeated string department/education/region label once and referencing it by a small integer code, instead of repeating full strings across 2,000 rows.

---

## Task 5 — Descriptive statistics and skewness

Skewness of all numeric columns, sorted by absolute value:

| Column | Skew |
|---|---|
| **distance_from_office_km** | **1.913** |
| monthly_hours | 0.922 |
| num_projects | 0.457 |
| salary | 0.062 |
| years_experience | 0.047 |
| performance_score | 0.028 |
| age | −0.018 |

**Most skewed: `distance_from_office_km` (skew = 1.91), positively skewed.** A positive skew means the distribution has a long right tail — most employees live relatively close to the office, but a smaller number commute from far away, pulling the tail out to the right (max = 57.6 km against a median of 5.6 km).

**Consequence for mean-imputation:** because the right tail is long, the **mean** (7.73 km) sits noticeably above the **median** (5.61 km) — the mean is dragged upward by the small number of long-distance commuters, so it doesn't represent a "typical" employee's commute. Filling missing values with the mean would systematically overstate how far a typical employee lives from the office; the median is the safer choice here (this is exactly what was applied in Task 2).

---

## Task 6 — Outlier detection (IQR method)

| Column | Q1 | Q3 | IQR | Lower bound | Upper bound | Outliers | % of rows |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| distance_from_office_km | 2.55 | 10.15 | 7.61 | −8.86 | 21.56 | **125** | 6.2% |
| monthly_hours | 106.50 | 178.03 | 71.53 | −0.79 | 285.31 | **57** | 2.9% |

Outliers were **not dropped**. For `distance_from_office_km`, the upper-bound outliers (>21.56 km) are plausible, real commute distances, not data-entry errors — dropping them would throw away genuine variation. For `monthly_hours`, values above ~285 hrs/month likely represent real overtime-heavy employees.

**Plan for Part 2:** these values will be **retained but their influence controlled through scaling** — `StandardScaler` (used in Part 2's leak-free preprocessing) reduces the disproportionate weight raw large values would otherwise have in models like Logistic/Linear Regression. Tree-based models in Part 3 (Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting) are largely insensitive to this kind of outlier by construction, since they split on rank/threshold rather than raw magnitude, so no additional capping is planned there.

---

## Task 7 — Visualizations

All six plots are saved in `plots/`.

1. **`01_line_salary.png`** — Salary by row index (first 200 rows). Since the data has no time dimension, this simply confirms rows are i.i.d. (no obvious trend or drift across the index), as expected for a dataset without a time component.
2. **`02_bar_mean_salary_by_department.png`** — Mean salary by department. Engineering has the highest mean salary (~$94.3K), HR the lowest (~$79.3K) — consistent with the department pay differentials built into the data-generation logic.
3. **`03_histogram_most_skewed.png`** — Histogram of `distance_from_office_km`. The shape is a classic right-skewed / long-tail distribution: a tall peak near 0–5 km that decays steadily, with a thin scatter of bars stretching out past 30 km.
4. **`04_scatter_experience_salary.png`** — `years_experience` vs `salary`. **Interpretation:** a clear positive, roughly linear relationship (Pearson r = 0.878) — each additional year of experience is associated with meaningfully higher salary, with moderate scatter around the trend (department/education/noise account for the spread).
5. **`05_box_salary_by_education.png`** — Salary by education level. **Interpretation:** median salary rises steadily with education (High School $77,983 → Bachelors $84,321 → Masters $90,483 → PhD $103,439). Spread (std) is similar across all four groups (~$21.6K–$22.3K), so education shifts the *center* of the salary distribution without meaningfully changing its *spread*.
6. **`06_correlation_heatmap.png`** — Full correlation matrix heat map (see Task 8 discussion below).

---

## Task 8 — Correlation heat map: highest-correlation pair

**Highest |correlation| pair: `age` and `years_experience`, r = 0.959.**

This is very unlikely to be a simple causal relationship in either direction — age doesn't directly *cause* years of experience, and vice versa. The more plausible explanation is a **third (unmeasured) variable: career-start age combined with elapsed time**. Both `age` and `years_experience` are driven by "how long ago this person started their career and how much time has passed since" — someone who started working at 23 will always have `years_experience ≈ age − 23`, by definition of how the two quantities are computed. So the strong correlation reflects a shared underlying driver (time since career start) rather than one variable causing the other.

(`salary` also correlates strongly with both `age`, r = 0.857, and `years_experience`, r = 0.878 — plausibly explained by the same underlying "career tenure" driver rather than age itself causing higher pay.)

---

## Task 8a — Imputation strategy comparison (mean vs median)

Computed for the two highest-skewness numeric columns, **before** any imputation was applied to them:

| Column | Mean | Median | Skew |
|---|---|---|---|
| distance_from_office_km | 7.726 | 5.610 | 1.913 (positive) |
| monthly_hours | 148.168 | 139.550 | 0.922 (positive) |

Both columns are **positively skewed**, so in both cases the mean is pulled *upward* by a minority of high-value rows (long commutes / heavy-overtime months), making the mean systematically higher than what a typical row actually looks like. **The median was chosen for imputation for both columns**, consistent with the Task 2/5 reasoning. After filling, `isnull().sum()` confirms **0 remaining nulls** in both columns.

---

## Task 8b — Spearman vs Pearson

Top 3 column pairs by `|Spearman − Pearson|`:

| Pair | Pearson | Spearman | \|diff\| |
|---|---|---|---|
| distance_from_office_km ↔ monthly_hours | 0.017 | 0.045 | 0.028 |
| performance_score ↔ distance_from_office_km | −0.002 | −0.029 | 0.027 |
| distance_from_office_km ↔ num_projects | −0.018 | −0.005 | 0.013 |

**Interpretation:** in all three pairs, both the Pearson and Spearman values are very close to zero (< 0.05 in magnitude) — these differences, while the largest in the matrix, are not meaningfully different from noise. None of these pairs show |Spearman| substantially exceeding |Pearson| in a way that would indicate a real monotonic-but-non-linear relationship; the small absolute differences are consistent with these variables being essentially uncorrelated (they were generated independently of one another). **For Part 2 feature-selection guidance, Pearson correlation will be relied on** as the primary signal, since the strongest real relationships in this dataset (`age`, `years_experience` with `salary`) are already well-captured linearly, and none of the low-correlation pairs show evidence of a disguised non-linear relationship that Spearman is uniquely surfacing.

---

## Task 8c — Grouped aggregation (salary by department)

| Department | Mean | Std | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering | 94,316.83 | 21,714.61 | 562 |
| Finance | 90,043.27 | 23,875.52 | 271 |
| HR | 79,316.95 | 22,155.88 | 197 |
| Marketing | 82,535.60 | 21,311.14 | 282 |
| Sales | 81,897.15 | 22,646.31 | 453 |
| Support | 80,999.09 | 23,482.61 | 235 |

- **Highest mean:** Engineering ($94,316.83)
- **Highest std:** Finance ($23,875.52)
- **Is high within-group std a concern?** Yes, somewhat — every department has a std around $21K–$24K, roughly a quarter of the mean itself. That means department alone leaves a lot of unexplained spread within each group (individual factors like experience, education, and performance still swing salary substantially even for two people in the same department) — department is a useful feature but not sufficient on its own to predict an individual's salary precisely.
- **Ratio of highest to lowest group mean:** 94,316.83 / 79,316.95 = **1.189**. A ~19% gap between the best- and worst-paid department on average is a real but moderate signal — enough that `department` is worth including as a predictive feature in Part 2, but not so large that it would dominate the model on its own.

---

## Files in this folder

- `generate_raw_data.py` — generates `data/raw_data.csv` (the "client" raw file)
- `eda_pipeline.py` — full Task 1–8c pipeline; run top-to-bottom, no arguments
- `data/raw_data.csv` — raw input
- `cleaned_data.csv` — cleaned output (2,000 rows × 11 columns), used by Parts 2 and 3
- `plots/` — all 6 required visualizations (PNG)
- `run_log.txt` — full console output from the last verified run

**To reproduce:** `python3 generate_raw_data.py && python3 eda_pipeline.py`
